data.title: Diversified Energy initiated Overweight at Stephens on valuation vs. natural gas peers
data.description:
Diversified Energy (DEC) up 1.9% in Thursday's trading as Stephens initiated coverage with an Overweight rating and $24 price target, saying the shares are valued at a wide discount to its natural gas group based on enterprise value-to-estimated 2027 EBITDA and free cash flow-to-enterprise value.

With a differentiated business model focused on the acquisition and optimization of proved developed producing assets, Diversified (DEC) is well-positioned to continue to generate consistent, double-digit growth and strong returns, Stephens analyst Mike Scialla said.

Portfolio optimization through joint ventures with operating partners or divestitures to E&P companies looking for drilling inventory could boost cash flow, Scialla said, looking for Diversified (DEC) to continue to repurchase shares while paying a ~7% annualized fixed dividend yield, the highest in Stephens' natural gas peer group.

While Diversified's (DEC) leverage is higher than the peer average of 1.0x, most of the debt is in the form of asset-backed securities that are non-recourse to the parent company, the analyst said; in addition to mitigating risk at the parent level, the investment-grade-rated ABS notes areamortized ratably and are underpinned by predictable cash flows.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase released from OCC order on trade surveillance
data.description:
The Office of the  Comptroller of the Currency ended a consent order against JPMorgan Chase (JPM) on Thursday that had been placed on the bank in 2024.

The regulator said the order was no longer needed due to the safety and soundness of the bank and its compliance with laws and regulations.

The consent order (AA-EC-2023-50) said JPMorgan Chase's (JPM) trade surveillance program had "certain deficiencies that have compromised its effectiveness"  and led to "unsafe or unsound practices."

Specifically, the OCC said JPMorgan (JPM) had failed to "surveil billions of instances of trading activity on at least 30 global trading venues."

Under the order, the bank was required to appoint a compliance committee and an examiner-in-charge to monitor and oversee the bank's compliance with the order. It also was required to submit an action plan to remediate the deficiencies and to provide progress reports on a quarterly basis. Furthermore, the order restricted JPMorgan from joining new trading venues until the examiner-in-charge provided it with a determination of no supervisory objection.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) stock rose 1.0% in Thursday afternoon trading.

data.title: Citizens outlines $450M Reimagine the Bank run-rate by end of 2028 while projecting 4Q 2027 NIM of 3.30%-3.50%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Citizens Financial Group (CFG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're pleased to start the year off strong, notwithstanding geopolitical tensions and uncertainty in the macro environment. We delivered good financial performance in a seasonally soft quarter with year-over-year EPS growth of 47%, positive operating leverage of 7% and NIM expansion of 24 basis points." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Bruce Van Saun)

  • "The Private Bank and Wealth business showed further growth in customers, balance sheet and profitability, now accounting for roughly 10% of our pretax income while delivering an ROE in excess of 25%." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun)

  • "Reimagine the Bank is off to a solid start, and we reaffirm our $450 million P&L target by the end of 2028. We estimate about $100 million in 2026 exit run rate benefits at this point." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun)

  • "We delivered EPS of $1.13 for the first quarter with ROTCE of 12.2%. Results were paced by strong NII, reflecting both continued net interest margin expansion and solid loan growth." (Executive VP & CFO Aunoy Banerjee)

  • "We also delivered our best-ever first quarter fee results, led by strong performance in our commercial bank." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "We're the #1 originator in the United States in home equity lending with an incredibly strong risk profile, low LTVs, strong FICO scores, all depository relationship customers." (President and Head of Consumer, Private Banking & Wealth Brendan Coughlin)

Outlook

  • The company’s EPS and revenue guidance comparison to analysts’ estimates was not included because the provided estimates data did not use the transcript-required quarter format.

  • "We provide our outlook for the second quarter. We expect net interest income to be up in the range of 3% to 4%, driven by continued expansion in net interest margin and earning asset growth." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "Noninterest income is expected to be up 3% to 5%, led by Capital Markets with some risk if market volatility moves higher." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "We are projecting expenses to be stable to up 1%, incorporating a step-up in implementation costs associated with Reimagine the Bank and continued investment in other key business initiatives." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "Looking out further, we see a clear path to achieving our 16% to 18% ROTCE target by the end of '27." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s macro framing that "project[ed] 2 more Fed rate cuts," management in Q1 emphasized a higher-for-longer possibility and said it was "very confident on getting to the NIM and the NII outlook that we gave." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

Financial Results

  • "We delivered EPS of $1.13 for the first quarter with ROTCE of 12.2%." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "Net interest income was up 1.6% linked quarter... our margin improved 7 basis points to 3.14%." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "Capital Markets performance demonstrated the strength and diversity of the franchise with fees up 34% year-over-year and down 4% compared with the strong fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "We returned about $500 million to shareholders in the first quarter, with $198 million in common dividends and $300 million of share repurchases." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • "Credit continues to trend favorably with net charge-offs coming in at 39 basis points, down from 43 basis points in the prior quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

Q&A

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.: Asked how Q1 capital markets performed vs. expectations and about pipeline pull-through; CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun said "we're pleased with the performance" and added "we may be in a situation where our pipelines are very full" and "we're certainly not taking our numbers down for the year."

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.: Asked about private credit portfolio credit quality and growth appetite; CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun said Citizens reviewed exposure "at a granular level" and feels "structurally well protected," while Executive VP & Head of Commercial Banking Theodore Swimmer said, "we really haven't seen a decrease in appetite."

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked how NII/NIM trends if rates stay higher for longer; CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun said a higher-rate scenario is "modestly positive," and Executive VP & CFO Banerjee highlighted time-based drivers, saying the remaining year includes "another 12 basis points" of benefit from swaps/non-core that is "not rate dependent."

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked about capital proposals, RWA benefit, and ERBA adoption; CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun said proposals could mean "a 10%-ish reduction in risk-weighted assets" and "in excess of 100 basis points" of CET1 improvement, and called ERBA "a lot of work" with benefits depending on treatment of "investment-grade credit."

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about confidence in loan growth drivers and utilization; CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun said growth is "pretty broad-based," and Executive VP & Head of Commercial Banking Swimmer said customers are "spending money on CapEx" driving utilization higher.

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked whether Reimagine the Bank/AI could accelerate; President Coughlin said, "we are moving very, very fast," and outlined call-center milestones: "by the end of the year, we should have 25% of our calls answered by non-humans" and "ramp in '27 to 50%."

  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: Asked about Private Bank growth mix and yields; President Coughlin said growth was more mortgage/multifamily this quarter due to lower PE utilization and said, "we remain in the range of north of 400, 425 basis points of net spread between our loan yields and our deposit costs."

  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about CCAR/SCB relief and CET1 targets; CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun said Citizens may reassess capital "probably... a '27 event" and not in 2026.

  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked about New York branch strategy and deposit opportunity; CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun said the bank expects to "open more retail branches in Manhattan" and framed deposit upside as "another $20 billion to $30 billion of deposits" over a decade if growth increases.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing on volatility, capital/regulatory uncertainty, and confidence in pipelines, reflected in questions on "confidence in pull-through" (Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.) and "magnitude of the benefit" from capital proposals (Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, with repeated language such as "we remain confident" (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee) and "we're certainly not taking our numbers down" (CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun), while acknowledging uncertainty tied to "the war" and market volatility.

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more upbeat macro setup (including "We project 2 more Fed rate cuts"), Q1 carried a more cautious posture around geopolitical risk while keeping full-year ranges intact, including "our full year outlook remains broadly in line with the guide we provided in January." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 emphasized execution progress and external risk management, with CEO & Chairman of the Board Van Saun highlighting "geopolitical tensions" and saying Citizens will stay on its investment plan "unless the macro takes a meaningful turn for the worst," versus Q4’s framing that "the macro environment for 2026 should be favorable."

  • Reimagine the Bank messaging shifted from launch-and-scope in Q4 to early operating specifics in Q1, including President Coughlin’s disclosure of "over $30 million in projected vendor saves" and the call-center/engineering AI deployment metrics.

  • Commercial Banking leadership changed on the call from Q4’s "Chair of Commercial Banking" Donald McCree to Q1’s "Head of Commercial Banking" Theodore Swimmer, who emphasized "more complex transactions" and M&A mandates building despite volatility.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Heightened geopolitical tensions" and "an increase in market volatility" were cited as risks to fee performance, with CFO Banerjee noting Q2 Capital Markets has "some risk if market volatility moves higher." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

  • Capital Markets timing risk was acknowledged as deal flow moved across months, with Executive VP & Head of Commercial Banking Swimmer noting transactions were "pushed... into April" due to volatility.

  • Management pointed to ongoing monitoring of credit and macro scenarios, with CFO Banerjee stating the allowance forecast "contemplates a mild recession" and reflects "the potential impact of higher energy prices." (Executive VP & CFO Banerjee)

Final Takeaway

Citizens’ management described Q1 as a strong start powered by net interest margin expansion, record first-quarter fee performance, and continued Private Bank growth, while reiterating key multi-year targets including Reimagine the Bank’s $450 million run-rate ambition by end of 2028 and a path to 16% to 18% ROTCE by end of 2027. Management kept the 2026 framework broadly intact but repeatedly tied near-term variability to war-driven volatility, with a Q2 setup that called for higher net interest income and noninterest income alongside stable-to-slightly higher expenses and continued capital return.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kering, Google aiming to launch Gucci smart glasses in 2027
data.description:
Kering (PPRUY) (PPRUF) CEO Luca de Meo said his company and Alphabet's Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) hope to launch Gucci-branded smart glasses next year.

During an interview with reporters on Thursday, de Meo said the Gucci glasses will probably be launched in 2027, according to Reuters. 

Reuters added that the venture is part of a broader effort to boost Gucci sales and scale up Kering's eyewear and jewelry lines.

Other smart glasses developers include Meta (META) and EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) (ESLOY), which market smart glasses under the Ray-Ban and Oakley brands, Amazon (AMZN), and Snap (SNAP). Google also has a smart glasses partnership with Warby Parker (WRBY).

data.description:

  • Forward yield 3.81%
  • Payable May 15; for shareholders of record May 1; ex-div May 1.
  • See DGICB Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Kaiser Aluminum launched at Overweight with 'big things coming' in small-cap packaging
data.description:
Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) up 4.8% in Thursday's trading as KeyBanc initiated coverage with an Overweight rating and $170 price target, saying it expects EBITDA dollars and margin to improve solidly in 2026-27 as end markets continue to improve and the company more fully executes on its growth strategies at Warrick and Trentwood.

A richer mix and solid end market demand supports stronger conversion revenue and EBITDA margin contribution from Kaiser's (KALU) packaging business as Warrick hits its stride, and the company has already signed long-term packaging agreements associated with Roll Coat Line #4, KeyBanc's Samuel McKinney said.

With aerospace OEM production expected to run hot for the foreseeable future and semiconductor plate inventories leaned out and primed for a recovery over 2026-27, McKinney views the investment timing as prudent to get ahead of coming demand.

Section 232 tariffs of 50% on products made entirely or almost entirely of aluminum further support softer import levels and record high domestic pricing, after the average MWTP jumped ~35% Y/Y in 2025, with a larger percentage magnitude increase expected in 2026 before dynamics begin to level off in 2027, the analyst said.

data.title: Paramount Skydance's Ellison makes ambitious promise to movie theaters
data.description:
Paramount Skydance (PSKY) chief executive pledged his support to the struggling movie theater industry with a commitment to keep his movies in the theaters longer and pledged an ambitious number of new releases per year.

“The Paramount lot is alive again, and we couldn’t be more excited,” Paramount Skydance (PSKY) CEO David Ellison said to his Las Vegas audience. 

According to Variety, Ellison made a surprise visit to industry gathering CinemaCon and shared his vision for a combined Paramount Skydance-Warner Bros. Beginning in 2027, the studio will offer a 45-day exclusive window for releases from Paramount Pictures, extending the 30-60 days other studios are currently giving movie theater owners.   

Moreover, Ellison said his company will commit to a three-month period for their movies to remain on streaming video-on-demand platforms before moving over to Paramount+ and committed to a minimum of 30 films per year for theatrical release once the Paramount-Warner Bros. merger is completed.

The merger of Paramount Skydance (PSKY) with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) is expected to be completed in the third quarter of 2026.

data.title: Abbott stock slips on guidance cut, ranks 10th in healthcare equipment Quant ratings
data.description:
Abbott Laboratories (ABT) kicked off Q1 2026 earnings season with a mixed report, where revenue grew ~8% year-over-year to $11.2 billion, beating the consensus by $160 million, driven primarily by a standout 13% growth in its MedTech division, marking the company's first top-line beat in two quarters.

The headline, however, was a lowered full-year EPS outlook, with 2026 adjusted diluted EPS now projected at $5.38–$5.58, down from the prior range of $5.55–$5.80, as the $21 billion acquisition of cancer diagnostics firm Exact Sciences introduced $0.20 per share of dilution, sending shares lower in trading.

Despite the revenue beat, Abbott ranks last on the following list of top 10 large-cap U.S. healthcare equipment stocks by Seeking Alpha Quant Rating, carrying a Hold score of just 2.86, reflecting headwinds from its diluted guidance, underperforming Nutrition segment, and compressed gross margins.

The following list ranks the top 10 U.S. healthcare equipment stocks by Seeking Alpha Quant Rating, a system grading stocks on valuation, growth, momentum, and profitability, with only Globus Medical (GMED) and Edwards Lifesciences (EW) earning bullish ratings above 3.5, while the remaining eight names, including Abbott, sit firmly in Hold territory or below.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings system grades stocks based on their relative performance across critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale of 1 to 5, with any score of 3.5 or above considered bullish and any score of 2.5 or below considered bearish.

Here is the list:

  1. Globus Medical, Inc. (GMED), Quant Rating: 4.71

  2. Edwards Lifesciences Corporation (EW), Quant Rating: 4.31

  3. GE HealthCare Technologies Inc. (GEHC), Quant Rating: 3.32

  4. Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (ISRG), Quant Rating: 3.30

  5. Stryker Corporation (SYK), Quant Rating: 3.10

  6. Penumbra, Inc. (PEN), Quant Rating: 3.08

  7. ResMed Inc. (RMD), Quant Rating: 3.07

  8. Insulet Corporation (PODD), Quant Rating: 2.96

  9. DexCom, Inc. (DXCM), Quant Rating: 2.90

  10. Abbott Laboratories (ABT), Quant Rating: 2.86

data.title: Aerospace gains momentum while defense turns cautious ahead of Q1 earnings: Baird
data.description:
Aerospace and defense companies are heading into first-quarter earnings season with diverging narratives, as robust aerospace demand contrasts with more tempered expectations across defense and government services, according to a new research report from Baird.

The firm expects “mixed” results for defense contractors in early 2026, while aerospace names continue to benefit from favorable sentiment, strong order books and production tailwinds.

Aerospace leads on demand strength

Baird analysts led by Peter Arment in the April 16 report said investor positioning increasingly favors original equipment manufacturers over aftermarket suppliers, citing sustained global flight activity and long-cycle production visibility.

Howmet Aerospace (HWM) was highlighted as a top pick, supported by strong demand in both commercial aerospace and defense markets. The company’s backlog extends well into the next decade, reinforcing confidence in long-term growth.

More broadly, aerospace suppliers are benefiting from rising aircraft build rates, increased maintenance activity and steady recovery in international travel. Even amid geopolitical tensions, global flight data has remained resilient, growing roughly 2% year over year, according to channel checks referenced in the report.

Defense spending strong, but timing matters

While the macro backdrop for defense remains constructive, near-term results may appear uneven due to timing-related factors, including fewer working days in the quarter, prior-period pull-forwards and disruptions tied to government funding delays.

Still, the long-term outlook is underpinned by what Baird describes as a “$1.5 trillion Department of Defense budget environment,” which continues to support investment in next-generation systems and munitions.

Programs tied to missile defense, hypersonics and nuclear modernization are driving multi-year demand. For example, missile production across several platforms is expected to grow two- to four-fold under new framework agreements, benefiting suppliers across the ecosystem.

Stock picks and earnings outlook

Baird identified several high-conviction names with potential to outperform earnings expectations, including Curtiss-Wright (CW), Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and Mercury Systems (MRCY).

Among large-cap contractors, most companies are expected to report results in line with consensus, with modest upside in select cases. RTX (RTX) is projected to post a slight earnings beat driven by record demand at its Raytheon segment, while Lockheed Martin (LMT) may see weaker free cash flow in the quarter due to billing timing issues.

Northrop Grumman (NOC) is expected to deliver steady results, supported by continued progress on key programs such as the B-21 bomber and Sentinel missile system.

Government disruptions

Temporary disruptions tied to government shutdowns and funding delays are also expected to weigh on some companies, particularly those with exposure to civilian agencies.

For instance, CACI International (CACI) and Leidos Holdings (LDOS) are both expected to see modest near-term impacts from Department of Homeland Security funding delays, though analysts view these as timing issues rather than structural concerns.

Similarly, several companies face margin pressure from tariff-related costs, supply chain dynamics or ramping capital expenditures tied to new programs.

Growth shifting to agile players

One notable theme in the report is a shift in defense procurement toward more agile companies willing to invest ahead of demand, rather than relying on traditional government-funded development models.

Baird said this dynamic favors small- and mid-cap defense hardware firms, particularly those investing aggressively in research and development and capacity expansion.

Outlook: Back-half weighted recovery

Despite first-quarter noise, analysts expect earnings and cash flow to improve as the year progresses, with many companies guiding to stronger second-half performance.

Free cash flow generation, in particular, is expected to be heavily weighted toward the back half of 2026, driven by profit growth and normalization of working capital dynamics.

Overall, the sector remains supported by durable demand drivers, even as companies navigate short-term operational variability.

“The setup supports buying pullbacks,” Baird analysts wrote, emphasizing that long-term growth trends in both aerospace and defense remain intact.
